narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 50.7% chance that you would not pass—crossing the threshold we use to protect the program's success rate. This prediction drew heavily from your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.60 and LSAT score of 34, both notably below the averages of 3.24 and 37.27 seen among students who went on to pass the bar. Your Year 1 and Year 3 decile rankings of 3 also played a key role, signaling performance in the lower third of your peers, compared to typical passers who ranked around the 6th decile. Among the factors the model weighed most strongly, your LSAT score stood out as the top influence, actually working in your favor by suggesting a somewhat stronger potential for success than the model might have expected otherwise. However, your Year 1 decile ranking had the strongest push toward predicting failure, as it indicated early academic struggles that often correlate with bar exam challenges. Your upper-middle family income was a smaller but still notable factor tilting against you, since while it's common among passers (about 45% of them), the model saw it as less protective here given your other metrics. Overall, these elements combined to paint a riskier picture than the profiles of accepted students, who typically show higher grades, stronger test scores, and top-decile school performance. Full-time enrollment plans align well with most successful students, but couldn't offset the core academic signals. It's understandable to feel disappointed, especially with your background and dedication evident in pursuing this path. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your undergraduate record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and seeking targeted bar prep resources early. Many students in similar positions have improved their profiles and succeeded—reach out to admissions for feedback on reapplying stronger next cycle. Your journey isn't over; this is just data pointing to areas for growth.
